## Factor Models

Factor models constitute one of the most influential frameworks in modern
finance because they provide a parsimonious representation of the drivers of
asset returns. Instead of modelling each asset independently, factor models
assume that co-movements in returns can be explained by exposure to a smaller
set of common risk factors. In their most general form, they decompose returns
into a systematic component (explained by factors) and an idiosyncratic
component (asset-specific residual noise).

A generic linear factor specification may be written as:

$$
r_{i,t} - r_{f,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i^\top f_t + \varepsilon_{i,t},
$$

where $r_{i,t}$ denotes the return of asset $i$ at time $t$, $r_{f,t}$ is the
risk-free rate, $f_t$ is a vector of factor realizations, $\beta_i$ represents
factor sensitivities (loadings), $\alpha_i$ is an intercept term, and
$\varepsilon_{i,t}$ is the residual return not explained by the selected
factors. This structure is attractive because it supports risk decomposition,
portfolio construction, performance attribution, and hypothesis testing in a
single analytical framework.

Historically, the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) can be interpreted as a
single-factor model in which excess returns are explained by exposure to the
market portfolio. Although CAPM played a foundational role in asset pricing, a
substantial empirical literature documented systematic deviations from its
predictions, including size, value, momentum, and profitability-related
effects. This motivated the development of multi-factor models, which expanded
the explanatory space while retaining tractability.

Two broad traditions of factor modelling can be distinguished. The first is
theoretical or economic factor modelling, in which factors are motivated by an
asset-pricing theory. A prominent example is the Arbitrage Pricing Theory
(APT), which posits that expected returns can be described by exposure to a set
of common factors under no-arbitrage conditions. APT is intentionally more
general than CAPM because it does not require a specific market portfolio and
allows multiple sources of priced risk. In practice, however, the theory does
not uniquely identify which factors should be used, and empirical
implementation depends on model specification choices.

The second tradition is empirical factor modelling, where factors are selected
based on observed regularities in the data. The Fama-French framework and its
extensions represent the most widely used examples in equity markets. These
models augment the market factor with variables intended to capture size, value,
profitability, and investment effects; the Carhart model further incorporates a
momentum factor. Their practical strength lies in improved return attribution
and in the ability to evaluate whether a trading strategy generates abnormal
performance after controlling for known systematic exposures.

In addition to economically motivated factors, statistical factor models use
dimensionality reduction techniques such as principal component analysis (PCA)
or latent variable methods to infer common drivers directly from return
covariance structure. These models are particularly useful in high-dimensional
settings where explicit economic interpretation is secondary to risk reduction
or de-noising. However, statistical factors may be unstable across samples and
are often more difficult to interpret than theory-based factors.

From the perspective of algorithmic trading, factor models are valuable in at
least four ways. First, they support the construction of benchmark portfolios
and risk controls, which is essential when evaluating more complex learning
systems. Second, factor exposures can be used as engineered state variables or
diagnostic signals in reinforcement learning pipelines. Third, they provide a
mechanism for distinguishing systematic risk-taking from genuine alpha
generation. Fourth, they offer a transparent intermediate layer between raw
market data and trading decisions, which can improve interpretability in
otherwise opaque systems.

Despite these strengths, factor models also exhibit limitations that are highly
relevant to this thesis. Factor loadings may vary over time, factor premia may
be regime-dependent, and transaction costs can erode apparent excess returns.
Moreover, linear factor structures may not adequately capture non-linear
interactions, threshold effects, or changing market microstructure conditions.
These limitations do not invalidate factor models; rather, they clarify why
they are best viewed as strong baselines and analytical tools, while adaptive
methods such as reinforcement learning may provide complementary capabilities in
dynamic trading environments.
